source_metadata.title_raw: Amy R. Webber - I-18-109 - June 26, 2018 - Long Beach

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Amy R. Webber 
Deputy City Attorney 
• Office of the City Attorney 
333 W. Ocean Boulevard, 11 th Floor 
Long Beach, CA 90802 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-18-109 
Dear Ms. Webber: 
June 26, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Sections 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact 
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes 
your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions 
should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
Because your request is general in nature and does not involve a particular governmental 
decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not 
provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 
QUESTIONS 
I. Does the Act prohibit the City of Long Beach's Fleet Manager from accepting an unpaid 
position as a board member of the Municipal Equipment Maintenance Association ("MEMA"), 
a nonprofit entity under S~ction 50l(c)(6) of the Internal Revenue Code? 
2. Is the Fleet Manager subject to reporting obligations under the Act with respect to a free meal 
provided to all attendees of a MEMA meeting by a vendor that sponsors that meeting? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. I-18-109 
Page No. 2 
l. No. Nothing in the Act prohibits the Fleet Manager from serving as an unpaid board member for 
MEMA. 
2. Yes. The Fleet Manager must report the meal on his or her Statement of Economic Interests as a 
gift from the vendor if the meal's value,.or if the value of the meal and other gifts received from 
that vendor during the calendar year, equals $50 or more. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
MEMA is a longstanding 50l(c)(6) nonprofit association of municipal fleet managers, made 
up mostly of managers from cities in the southern region of the State. MEMA holds bi-monthly 
meetings and occasional educational events and seminars on municipal fleet services. Topics of 
recent MEMA educational seminars-include "connected vehicles" and "renewable diesel fuel." 
MEMA's educational seminars are led by experts, who are sometimes vendors, sometimes not. 
Annual MEMA membership dues for agencies are $275, and $750 for a national level membership. 
MEMA meetings are sometimes sponsored by a Vendor associated with the fleet industry. 
Attendance is at no charge to MEMA members. The meeting sponsor occasionally provides lunch 
for attendees and pays any fee connected with the use of a venue for the meeting. 
The MEMA Board has seven members and two staff members. One of the staff members is 
responsible for administration, and the other is an "industry consultant" with ties to the fleet vendor 
community. The industry consultant arranges the vendor sponsorships that assist with funding 
MEMA's meeting and training seminars. MEMA Board Members run meetings, organize events, 
and generally participate in planning and decision making. They do not solicit vendors for 
• 
contributions. Although MEMA does not recommend or endorse products, it features various 
vendors and facilitates communication with members at its meetings. 
The City of Long Beach employs a Fleet Manager to oversee its Fleet Services Bureau. The 
Fleet Manager is responsible for the procurement and maintenance of City vehicles, including 
police cars, fire trucks, street sweepers, parking enforcement vehicles, and passenger cars for use by 
City employees. Among other duties, the Fleet Manager identifies appropriate vehicles for use by 
City departments, reviews specifications for procurements, and approves the use of City vehicles by 
City employees. 
ANALYSIS 
Private-Sector Employment of a Public Official 
The Act does not prohibit a public official from accepting additional private-sector 
employment, whether paid or unpaid. (Walls Advice Letter, No. 1-09-270.) However, an official's 
private-sector employment may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest unde.r the Act. 


File No. 1-18-109 
Page No. 3 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit an official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on one or more of the official's interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) Financial interests that may 
give rise to an official's disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 
87103. Under the Act, any of the following financial interests of an official may give rise to a 
disqualifying conflict of interest: 
• 
Any business entity in which the official has invested $2,000 or more (subdivision (a)) or in 
which the official holds any position of management (subdivision (d)). 
• 
Any real property in which the official has an interest worth $2,000 or more. (Subdivision (b).) 
• 
Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision. 
(Subdivision (c).) 
• 
Any donor, or intermediary or agent of a donor, of a gift or gifts aggregating $250 or more to 
the public official in the 12 months prior to the decision. (Subdivision (e).) 
• 
An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family 
members. 
Based on the facts presented, the Fleet Manager would not have a business interest in 
MEMA because a nonprofit association is not within the Act's definition of a "business entity" set 
forth in Section 82005, or a source of income interest in MEMA because the Fleet Manager would 
not receive income from MEMA for serving on the MEMA Board. Because we cannotconduct a 
comprehensive conflict of interest analysis under the Act without the identification of a particular 
governmental decision before the public official at issue, you should seek further advice if there is 
indication that a decision before the Fleet Manager would financially affect any of the financial 
interests of the Fleet Manager identified in Section 87103. 
Other bodies oflaw outside of the Commission's jurisdiction may also be implicated by the 
Fleet Manager's potential service on the MEMA Board.2 For example, Section 1126(c) requires a 
city to adopt a statement of incompatible activities providing notice to city employees of the 
activities the city determines to be inconsistent, incompatible, in conflict with, or inimical to the 
duties, functions, or responsibilities of the official or the city. You should review the City's 
statement of incompatible activities for further details, and may wish to consult the City Attorney 
regarding any other bodies of law that may apply. 
2 The Commission is only authorized to provide formal written advice regarding the Act and Section I 090. 
(See Section 83114 and Section 1097. I respectively.) Section I 090 generally prohibits an officer or employee of a state 
or local government agency from making or participating in the making of a public contract in which the officer or 
employee has a "financial interest." We do not analyze Section 1090 'because the facts presented provide no indication 
that a public contract is at issue. 


File No. 1-18-109 
Page No. 4 
Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit a public official, such as the Fleet 
Manager, from serving as an unpaid board member for a private-sector nonprofit association, such 
asMEMA. 
Reporting Obligations ~ith Respect to MEMA Meetings 
Section 82028(a) of the Act defines a "gift" as "any payment that confers a personal benefit 
on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." Section 
82030(a) of the Act defines "income" in pertinent part as follows: 
[A] payment received, including ... any salary, wage, advance, dividend, 
interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, including any gift of food or 
beverage, loan, forgiveness, or payment of indebtedness received by the filer, 
per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any 
person other than an employer, and including any community property interest 
in the income of a spouse. 
The Act prohibits an official from accepting gifts of more than $4 70 per year from a single source 
(Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2), but does not limit an official's income. 
To facilitate the identification of a public official's potential conflicts of interest, the Acf 
requires an official to file annually and upon assuming or leaving office, Statements of Economic 
Interests disclosing the official's investments, real property interests, and income. (Section 87200 et 
seq.) An official's income from a source is reportable if it equals $500 or more, and a gift to an 
official is reportable if it equals $50 or more, from a single source during the calendar year. 3 
(Section 87207(a)(l).) 
Under the Act, when a third party pays for an official's meal, that payment is generally 
considered to be a gift. (Section 82028; see Aranda Advice Letter, No. I-11-059(a).) However, in 
some cases, a payment for food in connection with work performed by the official for the source of 
the payment may be considered income rather than a gift. (See Section 82030(a).) Work performed 
by the Fleet Manager as a MEMA Board Member at a MEMA meeting would be for MEMA, not 
for the vendor sponsoring the meeting and providing the meal. 4 Therefore, the meal would not be 
income under the Act. 
A person that makes the gift to the official is the source of the gift unless that person is 
acting as an intermediary. (Regulation 18945.) A vendor that sponsors a MEMA meeting and 
provides a meal to an official is the source of the meal. Therefore, the meal is a gift, and the vendor 
that provides the meal is the gift's source. 
3 Note that a public official must aggregate multiple payments to determine ifhe or she has reached one of 
these reporting thresholds. Thus, the $50 threshold for gifts may be met by aggregating several smaller gifts received 
over the course of the calendar year from a single source. 
4 MEMA membership dues paid by the City to MEMA would not be consideration for the meal from the Fleet 
Manager to the vendor. 


File No. 1-18-109 
Page No. 5 
Thus, the Fleet Manager must report the meal from the vendor on his or her Statement of 
Economic Interests as a gift if the meal's value, or the value of the meal and other gifts received 
from the vendor during the calendar year, equals $50 or more. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
By: M~~ 
Counsel, Legal Division 
MFC:jgl 
5 You also ask if a sponsor's payment of the cost of a venue for a MEMA meeting would be subject to 
reporting by the Fleet Manager. Because that payment would be to MEMA, a 501(c)(6) nonprofit entity, rather than the 
Fleet Manager, and because the provision of the venue would not confer a personal benefit to the Fleet Manager, it is 
not a "gift" to the Fleet Manager under the Act.

I. Does the Act prohibit the City of Long Beach's Fleet Manager from accepting an unpaid

provided to all attendees of a MEMA meeting by a vendor that sponsors that meeting?
sections.conclusion:
l. No. Nothing in the Act prohibits the Fleet Manager from serving as an unpaid board member for 
MEMA. 
2. Yes. The Fleet Manager must report the meal on his or her Statement of Economic Interests as a 
gift from the vendor if the meal's value,.or if the value of the meal and other gifts received from 
that vendor during the calendar year, equals $50 or more.

MEMA meetings are sometimes sponsored by a Vendor associated with the fleet industry. 
Attendance is at no charge to MEMA members. The meeting sponsor occasionally provides lunch 
for attendees and pays any fee connected with the use of a venue for the meeting. 
The MEMA Board has seven members and two staff members. One of the staff members is 
responsible for administration, and the other is an "industry consultant" with ties to the fleet vendor 
community. The industry consultant arranges the vendor sponsorships that assist with funding
